_note: Points where Claude is NOT confident and wants a targeted human decision (#25 P3). Each flag is a specific choice with options + a recommendation — deliberately NOT 'review this whole line'. Confidence: low = I may well be wrong; medium = probably right, worth confirming.

resolutions._source: KA (native Nepali reader) 2026-07-17 + web research + Sanskrit dictionaries.

ne-register.note: KA understands all the colloquial draft; tatsama reads 'too intellectual' and doesn't sound Nepali. REVERSES my earlier recommendation. All body-part terms stay colloquial.

ne-legs-toes.note: खुट्टा covers leg+foot — that's just how Nepali works; keep the colloquial draft.

ne-shank-thigh.note: Sanskrit dicts confirm जङ्घा=shank/calf, ऊरु=thigh. पिँडुला/तिघ्रा correct. NOTE: KA mentioned a colloquial word sounding like 'sapro/saapro' for this region — unresolved, see council prompt.

ne-hips.note: KA confirms कम्मर = waist. Verse says कट्यां (कटि = waist/hip). So the ENGLISH tag 'Hips' is the mismatch, not the Nepali. Consider changing the English term to 'Waist' — pending.

v7-epithet.note: जिह्वाललनभीषणा = 'terrifying with lolling tongue' (लोलजिह्वा, classic Kali). English 'fearsome to foes' has no textual basis. Fix en, then ne/es follow. Corroborate wording via council before applying.

flags.title: Nepali register: colloquial or Sanskritic (tatsama)?
flags.why: This is the single biggest choice, and it cascades to ~10 terms. The Sanskrit being recited uses tatsama words (शीर्षे, भालं, ग्रीवां, उदरं, रक्त, मांस, अस्थि, जिह्वां). I drafted the body parts in everyday colloquial Nepali — more accessible to a general reader, but it drifts from the register of the text it sits beside.

options.label: Colloquial (what I drafted)
options.sample: टाउको · निधार · घाँटी · पेट · रगत · मासु · हड्डी · जिब्रो

options.label: Sanskritic / tatsama — mirrors the verse
options.sample: शिर · भाल · ग्रीवा · उदर · रक्त · मांस · अस्थि · जिह्वा

options.label: Mixed — tatsama where the verse uses it, colloquial elsewhere
options.sample: शिर · भाल · ग्रीवा · उदर, but रगत · हड्डी

flags.recommendation: sanskritic — a kavacam is liturgical; the tatsama forms match the Sanskrit on the same card and are well understood by Nepali devotees. Your टाउको-vs-शिर instinct was pointing right at this.

flags.title: Benedictive verb: रक्षा गरून् or रक्षा गरोस्?
flags.why: Every verse ends 'may X protect me'. I used गरून् (honorific/plural optative) throughout. Strict 3rd-person singular for one goddess is गरोस्. This is one grammatical choice repeated in all 14 verses — worth settling once.
flags.recommendation: garun — deities normally take the honorific in Nepali devotional prose, but I'd like this confirmed by someone who reads Nepali liturgy.

flags.title: Face and Mouth risk colliding (मुहार / अनुहार / मुख)
flags.why: I used मुहार for Face and मुख for Mouth. मुहार is colloquial; अनुहार is standard Nepali for face. And मुख covers BOTH face and mouth — verse 7 guards the face (मुखं) while verse 8 guards the mouth (वदनं), so these two tags must stay clearly distinct.
flags.recommendation: anuhar-mukh — अनुहार is unambiguous standard Nepali and leaves मुख free for the mouth.

flags.title: Hips = कम्मर (waist) or नितम्ब?
flags.why: Verse 9 reads कट्यां (kaṭi = waist/hip). I used कम्मर, which really means 'waist' in Nepali, while the English tag says 'Hips'. नितम्ब is specifically hips/buttocks.

options.label: कटि — tatsama, the verse's own word

flags.recommendation: kati — matches the verse and sidesteps the waist/hip mismatch entirely.

flags.title: खुट्टा means both 'leg' and 'foot'
flags.why: I used खुट्टा for Legs and खुट्टाका औँला for Toes. In Nepali खुट्टा covers leg AND foot, so both tags read loosely. Verse 11's Sanskrit is पादाङ्गुलीः (toes).
flags.recommendation: pada if you choose the Sanskritic register above (keeps it consistent); otherwise khutta is fine.

flags.title: Shanks / Thighs mapping (जङ्घा vs ऊरु)
flags.why: Verse 10 reads जङ्घोरू = जङ्घा + ऊरु. I read जङ्घा as shank/calf (→पिँडुला) and ऊरु as thigh (→तिघ्रा). I'm fairly confident, but some translators render जङ्घा as 'thigh', which would swap them.
flags.recommendation: current — जङ्घा is the shank/calf and ऊरु the thigh in classical usage.

flags.title: Intercardinals: आग्नेय/नैर्ऋत्य/वायव्य/ईशान or plain उत्तरपूर्व?
flags.why: I used the classical quarter names, which is exactly what the verses say (आग्नेय्यां, नैरृत्यां, वायव्यां, ऐशान्यां). Everyday Nepali would more likely say उत्तर-पूर्व etc.

options.label: आग्नेय · नैर्ऋत्य · वायव्य · ईशान (what I drafted)

flags.recommendation: classical — mirrors the verse text and is standard in ritual contexts.

flags.title: Verse 7: the epithet जिह्वाललनभीषणा looks mistranslated in the ENGLISH
flags.why: The existing English already hedges — 'the goddess—fearsome to foes'. But the compound parses as जिह्वा-ललन-भीषणा, 'terrifying with a lolling/rolling tongue' (a classic Kālī image), which has nothing to do with foes. My ne/es drafts inherited the English's reading, so if the English is wrong, all three are. This is the one place I think the EXISTING English needs fixing, not just my drafts.

options.label: Keep 'fearsome to foes'
options.sample: शत्रुका निम्ति भयङ्कर देवी / la diosa terrible para los enemigos

options.label: Re-read as 'terrifying with her lolling tongue'
options.sample: जिब्रो निकाली भयङ्कर देखिने देवी / la diosa terrible de lengua colgante

flags.recommendation: research — I'd corroborate this against the sources (as we did for #3) before changing it, because it also changes the English.

flags.title: Spanish '{n} seleccionadas' gender agreement
flags.why: The count suffix follows deidades (f), direcciones (f) and partes del cuerpo (f) — all feminine, so 'seleccionadas' agrees today. It would break if a masculine category is ever added (config makes that easy).
flags.recommendation: seleccionadas — correct for every current category; revisit only if a masculine one appears.
